title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation? A Complete Guide
content:
Phase II archaeological evaluation is one of the most critical steps in the federal cultural resource management (CRM) process. If your project has already completed a Phase I survey and identified an archaeological site on or near your project area, a Phase II evaluation determines whether that site is significant enough to warrant further protection or mitigation before construction can proceed.

Quick Answer: Phase II archaeological evaluation is a systematic testing process used to determine whether an identified archaeological site is eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP). It involves controlled excavations, artifact analysis, and a formal report that informs federal agency decisions under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act.

For federal agencies, state transportation departments, and private developers working on federally permitted or funded projects, understanding Phase II archaeological evaluation is essential. This evaluation directly shapes your project timeline, budget, and compliance obligations under the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA).

What Does Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Involve?

Phase II archaeological evaluation involves carefully controlled subsurface testing designed to assess a site's integrity, boundaries, and research potential. Archaeologists excavate systematically to gather enough data to make a well-supported NRHP eligibility recommendation. The work goes well beyond the shovel test pits typical of Phase I surveys.

Standard Phase II methods include hand-excavated test units (usually 1×1 or 1×2 meters), which allow detailed stratigraphic observation and artifact recovery. Excavations are placed across the site in a statistically defensible pattern to capture variability across different areas. All sediment is screened through hardware cloth (typically ¼-inch mesh) to recover artifacts, faunal remains, botanical materials, and other cultural evidence.

Beyond excavation, Phase II work encompasses detailed laboratory analysis. Recovered materials are catalogued, identified, and analyzed to interpret the site's age, function, and cultural affiliation. Special studies — such as radiocarbon dating, lithic analysis, ceramic typology, or faunal identification — may be conducted to answer specific research questions. All findings are synthesized in a formal evaluation report submitted to the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) and the lead federal agency.

When Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Required?

A Phase II evaluation is required when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site within or adjacent to a project's area of potential effects (APE), and that site cannot be avoided. The triggering mechanism is the need to assess NRHP eligibility as part of Section 106 compliance under the NHPA. Not every site found during Phase I requires Phase II work — only those with sufficient research potential to warrant further investigation.

The decision to proceed to Phase II is made collaboratively between the lead federal agency, the SHPO, and the project archaeologist. If a site is clearly disturbed beyond recovery — by deep agricultural plowing, previous construction, or erosion — it may be recommended as not eligible without further testing. However, when integrity is unclear or the site shows signs of significant buried deposits, Phase II evaluation becomes necessary.

Common project types that routinely trigger Phase II evaluations include highway expansions, pipeline corridors, transmission line projects, federal building construction, and federally funded housing developments. State and local projects involving federal permits or funding — such as Clean Water Act Section 404 permits — are also subject to Section 106 consultation and may require Phase II work.

How Does NRHP Eligibility Get Determined During Phase II?

National Register eligibility is assessed against four criteria established by the National Park Service. A site must demonstrate significance under at least one criterion: association with important historical events (Criterion A), association with important persons (Criterion B), distinctive architectural or archaeological characteristics (Criterion C), or potential to yield important information about history or prehistory (Criterion D). For most archaeological sites, Criterion D is the relevant standard.

Beyond meeting one of the four criteria, a site must also retain integrity — the ability to convey its significance through its physical character. Integrity is evaluated across seven aspects: location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association. A site buried beneath deep, undisturbed sediments may retain excellent integrity even if invisible from the surface, while a site subjected to repeated plowing may retain little.

The Phase II report presents an eligibility recommendation supported by the data collected. The lead federal agency, in consultation with the SHPO and any other consulting parties, then makes the formal eligibility determination. This determination drives all subsequent project decisions — including whether Phase III data recovery is required or whether the project can proceed with minor adjustments to avoid adverse effects.

What Happens After a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

After a Phase II evaluation, three primary outcomes are possible depending on the NRHP eligibility determination. If the site is found not eligible, the project can typically proceed without further archaeological constraints, subject to any standard monitoring or discovery conditions. If the site is found eligible but the project can be redesigned to avoid and protect it, a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or similar agreement document may formalize those avoidance measures.

If the site is found eligible and the project will adversely affect it, Phase III archaeological data recovery — sometimes called mitigation excavation — is required. Phase III involves large-scale excavation to recover the maximum amount of research data before the site is disturbed or destroyed. This is the most intensive and costly phase of the CRM process, making an accurate Phase II evaluation critically important to project planning.

Throughout this process, consultation with SHPO and other interested parties is ongoing. Tribes with ancestral connections to the project area are consulted under Section 106 and, for Native American human remains or cultural items, under the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (NAGPRA). Understanding these downstream obligations early helps agencies and developers plan realistic project schedules and budgets.

How Should Project Teams Prepare for Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

Effective preparation for Phase II archaeological evaluation begins before fieldwork starts. Project managers should provide the CRM firm with detailed project plans including construction footprints, grading extents, and utility corridors. Understanding the full extent of ground disturbance helps archaeologists design a testing strategy that addresses the site comprehensively and satisfies SHPO review standards.

Landowner coordination is equally important. Phase II excavations involve more extensive ground disturbance than Phase I shovel tests, and property owners or land managers must be informed and willing to allow access. In agricultural settings, timing fieldwork to avoid planting and harvest seasons minimizes conflicts and logistical complications.

Project teams should also build realistic schedule and budget contingencies for Phase II work. Laboratory analysis and report preparation typically take several weeks to months after fieldwork concludes, and SHPO review can add additional time before a formal eligibility determination is issued. Working with a qualified CRM firm early — one with demonstrated experience in geophysical survey and GIS-based site assessment — can help streamline the process and reduce costly surprises.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Phase II archaeological evaluation take?

A typical Phase II archaeological evaluation takes four to twelve weeks from fieldwork through final report delivery, depending on site complexity and size. Larger sites with extensive artifact assemblages or multiple cultural components require more laboratory analysis time. SHPO review after report submission can add four to eight additional weeks before a formal eligibility determination is issued.

How is Phase II different from Phase I archaeology?

Phase I archaeology identifies and records archaeological sites within a project area through systematic pedestrian survey and shovel testing. Phase II archaeology evaluates whether those sites are significant enough for National Register listing, using larger controlled excavations and detailed artifact analysis. Phase I asks "what is here?" while Phase II asks "does it matter historically or archaeologically?"

Who reviews the Phase II archaeological report?

Phase II reports are reviewed by the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO), the lead federal agency, and any other consulting parties identified during Section 106 consultation, including Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) when Native American interests may be affected. The federal agency makes the final NRHP eligibility determination after consulting with the SHPO.

Can a project proceed if a Phase II site is found eligible?

Yes, a project can proceed even if a Phase II evaluation finds a site eligible for the National Register, but the federal agency must resolve the adverse effect through the Section 106 process. Resolution options include project redesign to avoid the site, data recovery excavation (Phase III), or another agreed-upon mitigation measure documented in a Memorandum of Agreement between the agency and SHPO.

excerpt: Learn what Phase II archaeological evaluation involves, when it's required under Section 106, and how NRHP eligibility is determined for your CRM project.
